review_text:
I don't know how the keywords work. I've literally filled in with full of words but the arts aren't generated as what I wrote. It just gives random images of cat girls or whatever they call Nyanco but it draws surprisingly well! I guess the words are limited because it's free?

Even though the results aren't as what I expected but they are still satisfyingly good enough. But honestly it could've been better if it offers higher resolutions and at least a word relative result. Anyway it gives what I get for free but I liked it. Totally recommended.

Of course I've paid for DLC to support the devs and expect better cultural industry, if you catch my meaning :)

There's no sound and the DLC is expensive. But what other game allows you to browse, name, and give dialogue to AI-generated anime girls? There are only two drawbacks to Nyanco Impact:

- Risk of screenshotting every single image that's generated
- This game might be why Nyanco's hair went from silver to purple

Do you like kittens?, now can get your favorite kitties for free

And present your respect to the publisher

Get ready to read the best review you have ever seen.
Absolutely astonishing game. When starting the game you are greeted by a Catgirl and The Classic Chad logo Nyanco Impact. The drawing has been perfected to the utmost abilities capable of a human being (then again nyanco is god so this is an easy feat for them) The title of the game is simple yet luring in making you think what could happen next. In this game you get to have the option to input words that will specify the picture you get to see. The game is very intresting and easy to get into. WIth just a few clicks you can generate a Catgirl to your liking with a few words you choose. But before we go on further with this ad lets hear about todays sponsor. Nyanco. Nyanco is a fun Indie RPG type game where you go around with rather Strange Mechanics. With you having a bomb in your heart off to explode if you move a single inch to the right or left. But how would u know the bomb is there for sure? You could just move and see right but what if there actually is one and u die? Well thats the goal of the game try navigating the game without using Left or Right because you could explode or you could not. Thats up to you to find out. (Nyanco can be found in steam store)  Back to the Nyanco Impact review I gotta say this is an Impressive game from the almighty god Nyanco and it is not often Nyanco Dev games do come out but when they do they are usually fire. By buying the NSFW DLC for this game you can unlock rather explicit content 18+ when using the AI Drawing system. You can also give your Catgirl a name and dialogue for her to say. This is a rather rare mechanic in games but then again this is a Nyanco Dev Game (best game devs) so just another +1 on the rating 

And for the end of this review all I gotta say is game is 10/10 
my epic quote ---> ''Nyanco is god, Nyanco is life, Hail Nyanco.''
[h1]Before I played Nyanco Impact, I had a small pp, no friends, no gf, depression, and no life. These things havent changed, but the game is pretty good[/h1]
WHILE YOU CAN MAKE SOME ABSOLUTE VILE THINGS AND DOESN'T SEEM TO BLOCK VULGAR WORDS, IF YOU TRY TO SCREEN CAP IT WITH SHARE X,  YOUR TEXT WILL THANOS SNAP AND DISAPPEAR CAW CAW
Nyanyame nyanyajyuunyanya-do no nyarabi de nyakunyaku inyanyaku nyanyahan nyanyadai nyanynaku nyarabete nyaganyagame
It gave me an AI generated anime cat girl waifu. This is some black box magic.

Resolution is not native and sometimes stretched, but yes for the generated anime, the background looks like its being turned to another perspective.

In conclusion
Yes and yes and yes
Very good, hopefully the author can create a better work！
I don't think anybody asked for this specifically but by god did somebody out there get what they wanted.

If you're an AI Dungeon immigrate like me, you'll find AIdventure incredibly easy to get into and start making stories in no time at all. If not, the community hub has a great Guide that explains everything you need to get started.

AIdventure is the best offering in the field of "AI driven text adventures." No monthly fees, a wide variety of AI models including KoboldAI and Chat-GPT, all your stories are private until you share them yourself, and best of all, the developers care! Updates frequently, answers questions in a helpful manner, and all in all just great people that deserve respect!

'Nuff said!

Really awesome game! Really cool to just vibe to on 2nd monitor when playing the larger models because of the generation time. The only thing that would be nice is if it can finish the generation of text in a complete manner each time. Some models are better than that than others.

Amazing since it's a one time purchase unlike most AI text generation games.

I would really like to play this game, however I made the mistake of choosing an AMD GPU, which this game doesn't seem to support. : (

I can't really recommend it, mainly because I can't even play it due to my choice of GPU.. which honestly I regret even more now x.x

----- Update! -----

Leaving in the negative review from before just to show a contrast.

The Game Dev reached out to me and offered to help and they ended up figuring out a solution that now allows me to play this game. If anything, I recommend just because the dev themself seems to want to make this work and will work with their players to find solutions to problems with the game : )

I like it; custom stuff takes a lot to set up, but it is proving to be well worth the effort. Currently roaming around my own version of Gotham City, a little copy-paste work, a wiki here and there, toss it in the game lore book, and simmer!

There are some pre-generated adventures that are really cool, and you can modify those as well. Custom stuff can get as in-depth as the time you are willing to spend on creating the content. I have only had issues with one model so far; the other few I have used have been doing fine, and there is a good selection. All in all, this is turning out to be pretty cool.

You not only have total freedom, meaning you can control what the AI does (for example, how many words can it say if you click "Generate"), but you also have 14-16 different AIs. I have to admit, the AI you choose can sometimes be too...annoying, but you can FULLY customize what the AI says.

Want to have a story-rich, deep and sophisticated world/RP, where every turn generated leaves you begging to know more? Want to have a full blown out one-hand experience to the point where you don't feel your body? YOU HAVE IT.

The newest experimental branch HEAVILY optimized the bot, it takes a few SECONDS for it to generate text. I love you Lya mwah
Is it possible to add automatic Chinese translation, I would like to recommend it to my Chinese friends

[quote][b]Check out our [url=store.steampowered.com/curator/36364102-Horny-Hentai-Festival/]Steam curator[/url] , [url=www.youtube.com/curatorhhf/]YouTube channel[/url] and [url=twitter.com/hhfcurator]Twitter[/url]![/b][/quote]
Text adventure game with AI storyteller help. You can switch various AIs you like, describe the story, and can share each other via communities. Can feel tons of potential.

This is the perfect AI text game I was looking for.

Its basically AI Dungeon on your own PC without censorship with the character attributes and a 'TRY' prompt much like a dungeons and dragons table top style strength check. I became aware that one can download one of the GTP2 pre-trained models directly and just run the thing in a command window but its not very neat and you need to make use of various /commands. From what I understand this is basically a GUI wrapper with the same sort of functionality (at least now) with the addition of the previous mentioned TRY [attribute] text prompts ... which is really cool. You also generate a attribute sheet on a new character with the ability to randomize them all or manually input a value (the try system is based of percentile to your attributes value and it only comes into play when you do one of the challenges).

My system: i7-9700K factory OCed to 4.5ghtz, 32 GB of DDR5 ram, RTX 3060 12gb, installed SSD drive read/write of 559/524 with a random read/write of 368/297. The biggest one is a 25gb memory model that more or less maxes my ram and cpu but works well. The 16gb AI ones run without maxing my hardware so that is best for my system.

Another AI game I have and recomend is AI Rougelite on steam here which is a MUD-style game with AI image generation, items and skills/spells presented to be more of a dungeon crawler (also that runs off your GPU only). This one is more about stories and can run off your CPU and ram. I am VERY happy with each. This one is the PERFECT alternative to AI Dungeon with capabilities to perform a bit better then AI Dungeon's free Griffon model at the 25gb ones.

This one is new and in early access but it runs purely off GPT2 so it'll perform and run just like that. My hope is that the dev adds more custom bells and whistles to this as time goes on. Maybe expanding the TRY interaction system somehow. As it is now? You can run this out of the box in its current state just fine.

I give AIdventure a healthy 4.5 out of 5

It was already quite good for what I paid. But the new experimental branch has mad it 100 times better. It generates text much faster now that it splits the load between your CPU and GPU. It's a great alternative to AIDungeon and NovelAI at a good price and 100% guaranteed privacy since it's your own machine doing the work.

Pros:
Automatically downloads relevant GPT models from the internet. Nice interface.
Better the ai dungeon even if its a bit buggy

AIDventure,

Does some things that A.I. Dungeon doesn't let you do. And that's pretty cool.
One of those things is the "Ask another generation". This allows you to request another suggestion made by the system without losing the former ones. This means that you can just compare them to eachother and edit and pick the one you like the most.

Besides that, the pricing is okay for what you get and for what's possible so far.
Yes, you can do the same things as with all those other big shot engines, but it will take time for your low life rig to create the output. Just be patient and play around with the settings until you find something you can work with.

It's well worth buying as a budget bargain. A promising entry for anyone new to AI stories and adventuring. Developer has made a good start with this and has said that a tutorial and nicer interface is being worked on too. This would make this project a cheap and ideal beginner option that doesn't rely upon subscription services. I think over time this will make itself distinct as an option compared to vanilla KoboldAI and that will be a good thing for people to have increased options. Currently in Early Access, I look forward to hopefully updating this review with more good points on the features and abilities of this package in the future too.

Let me start this off by saying that I have known Lyaaaaa, been a mod for the discord and done the (admittingly improvable) German translations for a while now and I've used it for a long time before from itch, so I am probably a bit biased, but let me tell you that, for me personally, this has been my definitive alternative to Aidungeon and NovelAi if you don't want a subscription and cloud based solution.

AIDungeons practices are shady and NovelAI ist amazing but pricy. I'm not willing to spend 15 bucks a month for what it is.
AIdventure shifts the workload onto your computer, so there is no price to "rent" the software, allowing for a one-time purchase.
It has most features that I'd want for an AI-Story generator (and AI RPG if you prefer that, it can do both) that I want and with the lore book, most of what I've wanted is already in the game.
But that's not all, Lyaaaaa is extremely hard working, constantly adding new and unique features and fixing bugs and always has an open ear for users with suggestion or problems. For example the recent addition of the lorebook was originally an idea I pitched, because I felt it essential to make it generate more comprehensive stories, and a couple of months later Lyaaaaa had implemented a first draft for it.
And believe me when I tell you that there are even more awesome ideas floating around that will make this software even more unique.

The only other software comparable is KoboldAI and its various open source forks, and that's free and community driven. So why buy this and not just use a KoboldAI fork?
In my opinion with Lyaaaaa as a developer, who's constantly trying to improve performance and  the cool ideas floating and the ambition for the software to constantly grow and add more features, the money is well worth it for me and I can recommend this without a second thought.

You not only have total freedom, meaning you can control what the AI does (for example, how many words can it say if you click "Generate"), but you also have 14-16 different AIs. I have to admit, the AI you choose can sometimes be too...annoying, but you can FULLY customize what the AI says.

Want to have a story-rich, deep and sophisticated world/RP, where every turn generated leaves you begging to know more? Want to have a full blown out one-hand experience to the point where you don't feel your body? YOU HAVE IT.

The newest experimental branch HEAVILY optimized the bot, it takes a few SECONDS for it to generate text. I love you Lya mwah
Is it possible to add automatic Chinese translation, I would like to recommend it to my Chinese friends

[quote][b]Check out our [url=store.steampowered.com/curator/36364102-Horny-Hentai-Festival/]Steam curator[/url] , [url=www.youtube.com/curatorhhf/]YouTube channel[/url] and [url=twitter.com/hhfcurator]Twitter[/url]![/b][/quote]
Text adventure game with AI storyteller help. You can switch various AIs you like, describe the story, and can share each other via communities. Can feel tons of potential.

This is the perfect AI text game I was looking for.

Its basically AI Dungeon on your own PC without censorship with the character attributes and a 'TRY' prompt much like a dungeons and dragons table top style strength check. I became aware that one can download one of the GTP2 pre-trained models directly and just run the thing in a command window but its not very neat and you need to make use of various /commands. From what I understand this is basically a GUI wrapper with the same sort of functionality (at least now) with the addition of the previous mentioned TRY [attribute] text prompts ... which is really cool. You also generate a attribute sheet on a new character with the ability to randomize them all or manually input a value (the try system is based of percentile to your attributes value and it only comes into play when you do one of the challenges).

My system: i7-9700K factory OCed to 4.5ghtz, 32 GB of DDR5 ram, RTX 3060 12gb, installed SSD drive read/write of 559/524 with a random read/write of 368/297. The biggest one is a 25gb memory model that more or less maxes my ram and cpu but works well. The 16gb AI ones run without maxing my hardware so that is best for my system.

Another AI game I have and recomend is AI Rougelite on steam here which is a MUD-style game with AI image generation, items and skills/spells presented to be more of a dungeon crawler (also that runs off your GPU only). This one is more about stories and can run off your CPU and ram. I am VERY happy with each. This one is the PERFECT alternative to AI Dungeon with capabilities to perform a bit better then AI Dungeon's free Griffon model at the 25gb ones.

This one is new and in early access but it runs purely off GPT2 so it'll perform and run just like that. My hope is that the dev adds more custom bells and whistles to this as time goes on. Maybe expanding the TRY interaction system somehow. As it is now? You can run this out of the box in its current state just fine.

I give AIdventure a healthy 4.5 out of 5

It was already quite good for what I paid. But the new experimental branch has mad it 100 times better. It generates text much faster now that it splits the load between your CPU and GPU. It's a great alternative to AIDungeon and NovelAI at a good price and 100% guaranteed privacy since it's your own machine doing the work.

Pros:
Automatically downloads relevant GPT models from the internet. Nice interface.
Better the ai dungeon even if its a bit buggy

AIDventure,

Does some things that A.I. Dungeon doesn't let you do. And that's pretty cool.
One of those things is the "Ask another generation". This allows you to request another suggestion made by the system without losing the former ones. This means that you can just compare them to eachother and edit and pick the one you like the most.

Besides that, the pricing is okay for what you get and for what's possible so far.
Yes, you can do the same things as with all those other big shot engines, but it will take time for your low life rig to create the output. Just be patient and play around with the settings until you find something you can work with.

It's well worth buying as a budget bargain. A promising entry for anyone new to AI stories and adventuring. Developer has made a good start with this and has said that a tutorial and nicer interface is being worked on too. This would make this project a cheap and ideal beginner option that doesn't rely upon subscription services. I think over time this will make itself distinct as an option compared to vanilla KoboldAI and that will be a good thing for people to have increased options. Currently in Early Access, I look forward to hopefully updating this review with more good points on the features and abilities of this package in the future too.

Let me start this off by saying that I have known Lyaaaaa, been a mod for the discord and done the (admittingly improvable) German translations for a while now and I've used it for a long time before from itch, so I am probably a bit biased, but let me tell you that, for me personally, this has been my definitive alternative to Aidungeon and NovelAi if you don't want a subscription and cloud based solution.

AIDungeons practices are shady and NovelAI ist amazing but pricy. I'm not willing to spend 15 bucks a month for what it is.
AIdventure shifts the workload onto your computer, so there is no price to "rent" the software, allowing for a one-time purchase.
It has most features that I'd want for an AI-Story generator (and AI RPG if you prefer that, it can do both) that I want and with the lore book, most of what I've wanted is already in the game.
But that's not all, Lyaaaaa is extremely hard working, constantly adding new and unique features and fixing bugs and always has an open ear for users with suggestion or problems. For example the recent addition of the lorebook was originally an idea I pitched, because I felt it essential to make it generate more comprehensive stories, and a couple of months later Lyaaaaa had implemented a first draft for it.
And believe me when I tell you that there are even more awesome ideas floating around that will make this software even more unique.

The only other software comparable is KoboldAI and its various open source forks, and that's free and community driven. So why buy this and not just use a KoboldAI fork?
In my opinion with Lyaaaaa as a developer, who's constantly trying to improve performance and  the cool ideas floating and the ambition for the software to constantly grow and add more features, the money is well worth it for me and I can recommend this without a second thought.

If you're an AI Dungeon immigrate like me, you'll find AIdventure incredibly easy to get into and start making stories in no time at all. If not, the community hub has a great Guide that explains everything you need to get started.

AIdventure is the best offering in the field of "AI driven text adventures." No monthly fees, a wide variety of AI models including KoboldAI and Chat-GPT, all your stories are private until you share them yourself, and best of all, the developers care! Updates frequently, answers questions in a helpful manner, and all in all just great people that deserve respect!

'Nuff said!

Really awesome game! Really cool to just vibe to on 2nd monitor when playing the larger models because of the generation time. The only thing that would be nice is if it can finish the generation of text in a complete manner each time. Some models are better than that than others.

Amazing since it's a one time purchase unlike most AI text generation games.

I would really like to play this game, however I made the mistake of choosing an AMD GPU, which this game doesn't seem to support. : (

I can't really recommend it, mainly because I can't even play it due to my choice of GPU.. which honestly I regret even more now x.x

To simplify this review, in it's current state (Version 0.49) Back of Space is not worth the 10$ price tag. It lacks several features that its predecessor (OldTv) had.

I am a huge fan of the OLDTV franchise (if you can even call it that). I still stand by the fact that my best purchase ever was COLDTV (2$ DLC for OLDTV). When I heard of the release of Back of Space, I had what can only be described as a minor heart attack. 

OLDTV is free, Back of Space is 10$ and has less features than OLDTV, so I reccomend at least trying out the predecessor before getting this one. 

I have faith in the developers, but if you haven't played OLDTV and this is your first experience with this "franchise", play OLDTV first, you won't regret it.

At the time of writing, I'd rate Back of Space Version 0.49 around a 7/10, as long as the game gets consistent updates, I'm sure that it will soon overgrow its predecessor. 

Note: I noticed that the latest update added the settings menu, yet upon opening the game, there is no visible settings menu for me, so unless my computer is not loading something correctly, or the UI is messed up, then I don't exactly know what to do about the latest update. Bug or not, I still commend the devs for being active with the community and I look forward to further development, thank you.

All i can say in this early state that I trust the process of developement. 
Be patient guys. 

But from the gaming perspective: IM HOOKED

The style feels satisfying, The animations are flawless and the audio just makes everything perfectly balanced. Im really looking forward to the story and lore that goes along.

overwhelmed by how good this game looks like.

The videos playing in the background, the VHS effect, the bloom, everything...the sounds.
Everything is so freaking perfect I love it.

TL;DR: It's not a DIY base builder, but it is a game about conflicting narratives on commoning. 

I feel Common'hood is one of those games that will be forever polarizing. Part of it comes from the silly bugs like bad pathfinding and questionable game design decisions. However, even if that was fixed in the near future, it will still be a controversial title.

Contrary to people's expectations and the articles/reviews written about it thus far, this game is not supposed to be some base builder. Rather, you are supposed to figure out what you're supposed to do. This is implicit in the narrative, the lack of tutorials, and the NPCs you recruit. Everyone is unsure what to do next and that's the "meat" of the story, so to speak. There's no hand-holding or clear narrative/gameplay arc.

You're supposed to be fumbling about and trying to get things sorted out.

The entire game is oriented around the difficulty of building a commons. There's characters who will call you out for your productivism and the more you open up the warehouse, the more you cause the squatters within the place to be more susceptible to the police. Very few tasks are actually directly related to your overall progression; all of them have to do with the specific, individual needs of (prospective) members of your commons. You are all figuring out what it means to build a commons in spite of capitalism.

In other words, you're trying to organize [i]something[/i]. But you're not sure how and why you're doing it. This game wants you to interrogate yourself and your own goals.

The game is great, even if it is admittedly frustrating at times. If you jive with the game, you'll dig what it's trying to do. I describe it as a thinking exercise on what it means to build an anticapitalist commons, with the jank of [i]Pathologic[/i] or [i]S.T.A.L.K.E.R[/i]. It's a hard sell, but it's great for those who can handle what it demands from you.

The devs for this game committed one of the worst sins, when they released it while it was far from finished. Lots of spellings errors, performence issues, broken mechanics and worst of all quest breaking errors.But that was some months ago, since then severel bugfixes has been added, so the game seems fullt playable. It still has some clunky game mechanics in crafting and building, but it seems like you can now do everything, which you are supposed to do.So now I can review the game, that the Devs intened to create. It is a survival crafting game.. without any survival mechanics. So the game takes place  in a big abondonned factory, where your character can research stuff, gather resources, craft things and gather followers, with a gameplay revolving around the destiny of the homeless people living in the abononed factory and how you can create hope for them, by creating a community, where they can be useful and follow their dreams.The story might be too much social realism for some,, but I  find the stories and the development of the cummunity insteresting. But mostly I like the research part of the game with a wast research tree.There is no hand holding in the game, so finding unique items and realising which quests or research are needed to progress to some can be quite difficult. Which can be seen as an interesting challenge or just tedious depending on your patience and investment in the story.So this game(still) isn't prefect with some clunky game mechanics and also a lot of grinding in creating items. But if you like a down-to-earth building exploration game, with an interesting story and a lot of reasearch, you might like this game. If you expect your survival games to be of the size and quality of games like Subnautica and Satisfactory you might be dissapointed be this game, as it is much smaller and has hasn't the smooth gameplay of those.There will probably be more bugfixes in the future, so the mechanics might eventually be quite smooth, it is just a shame, that this was not done before release.

A weird metaphor of life and the world?

Don't get me wrong, it's not that I didn't like the game. On the contrary, I've been a huge fan of Block'hood (Plethora's previous work) and I've been eagerly awaiting this new product. It was a delight to find similarities between these two very different games.
While Block'hood is a neighbourhood builder, Common'hood is a survival game: you start as a girl who lost everything but her father's books and workbench, go to live in the occupied factory where your parents used to work, meet other squatters and team-up with them to create an alternative form of social construct.
The main focuses of the game are automating production and optimising energy, building better shelter for you and your friends, fulfilling their personal quests and exploring the whole factory to find out the truth of the terrible accident in which your parents lost their life.
Mechanics are simple (build things to build more things to automate building things). Underlying themes are complex.
And this is where I felt the game unfortunately fell short.
As a privileged white girl who studied engineering and owns a bunch of books, you get to bring "new energy" to the disillusioned community of other young squatters, and all it takes is a bit of studying and some scrap metals to create huge robotic arms that will print out brand new homes for the whole neighbourhood. I'm an optimistic by nature, but the level of socialist positivism is to blatant it got annoying even for me.
Still, I understand what they were trying to do and, don't get me wrong, I endorse the underlying political agenda. I only felt it should have been presented with a little more care to nuances, being such an important theme.
I'm not going to lie it is very buggy and power intensive, but it is truly one of my favorite games. It is a story-driven survival game.

If you like building, crafting, and exploring, don't miss out on this underrated gem.
It has some minor hiccups. They're minor.
Building: It's incredible. The most free-form building mechanic I have seen. You can build structures by placing foundations, frames and skins. Or not. You can, for instance, frame in a window in a wall of a building.
Crafting: Find resources to make everything from parts for machinery to materials for construction. The machinery parts make sense, but on a scale that keeps the crafting mechanic fun. Motors require gears, wires, and scrap metal, for instance. Wires are made from copper scrap. Gears are made from metal scrap. 
Research: There's a research tree! Unlock new areas to add useful stations to your growing community, or to improve your own or their ability to do things faster.
Automation: Create something and then set it to build order. Your community friends will build it for you. Tell them to farm, make 4x4s or other parts, or even conduct research for you.
Story: There's an underlying story to it - a reason why your character is going through this and deciding to help others. It keeps you exploring, crafting, building all to make their lives, and yours, better.
This is a gem. It has room for improvement. Would love to see more freedom in rotation while placing machines or designing structures. Would like to see some tool tips on inventory items to indicate how those items are crafted or found. But these are minor. 
This game is enjoyable, and if you like building and crafting games, you really shouldn't miss out on this one.

I give this game a thumbs up but with several caveats that only the developers can eliminate.

Common Hood is an original and intriguing game. It has a captivating storyline that draws the player in. That is probably its greatest strength.

There is much to create and the creative elements are quite broad and engaging. The AI characters are unique and provide options for customization.

Gameplay is mostly linear. It is not open world as most would understand that concept. The player may roam the areas they have quested into, but other areas may not be accessed until certain qualifications have been satisfied. And it's not like attaining a level before access, it's finding a book or clearing debris that requires a certain tool etc. It reminds me of Myst but with fewer hints. Also there's a political take that is wasted on gamers. We don't play to wrestle with the real world.

The linear access venture wouldn't be bad except there are few clues or stated requirements to guide the player to the next step. So there is a lot of floundering around trying to figure out what to do next and much of it is completely non-productive. Players will likely spend a lot of time in Steam Discussion and Discord to advance.

So why thumbs up if it is that confusing? Because it needn't be. The developers should listen to players who overwhelmingly agree about the vagueness of the pathway and give a more directed course. It's fine to hunt awhile as long as you know what you are hunting for. And that goes for most aspects of the game. Give the players more insight into what they are seeking and why.

So, once the player has opened a new area, they understand a bit more. But there are few clues about what to do next. The player just knows that there is something else to know or acquire but not how to get there.

So give it a try and let the developers know how they can provide the player with a better game. I hope they'll listen because they could have a hit.

Honestly, so far I like this game quite a lot, there's a lot to like about it, but unfortunately there are quite a few problems with it as well.

Pro's.
1. the building system is very nice overall and you can make some pretty nice buildings out of it, same with the blueprint system, although there are some minor bugs that can mess with it at times but overall its great.

2. I love the community aspect, being able to befriend other characters to help your community is great and learning more about them is amazing as well, and most of the missions help a lot with learning about the game progression

3. The crafting system pretty nice and serves its purpose well, although I with there was some kind of wishlisting system so you could see the required materials without being at the crafting station.

4. the general gameplay at least to me is very fun to just chill too, while it may be repetitive that's not necessarily a bad thing, plenty of games are extremely repetitive, like stardew valley, animal crossing, etc... and do it well, as does this in my opinion.

5. The demo gives you tons of content to mess with alone and is probably the only reason I ended up picking this game up because it does a great job showing you the basics of the game to make sure this kind of game is even for you, and I really appreciate that.

Con's 
1. The game currently is in a pretty unpolished and glitchy state, with some areas like the elevator and concrete hallway at launch not working correctly although I believe they fixed those issues, as well AI not working correctly, like in every area after the first I have not been able to make farming work at all because the community members just run into the water tank for the whole day when they are farmers, unable to actually get any water out of it, which once again they seem to be working on fixing these problems, which I really appreciate them fixing some of the issues that can plague this game sometimes.

2. The sheer amount of crafting stations get pretty ridiculous, with me being where I assume is about halfway through the main game at the glasshouse, I have over 20 crafting stations with many of them being completely un-needed, like why are  things such as the band saw and chop saw completely separate from the table saw and not just upgrades of some kind to the table saw itself? same as with things like the bench grinder, drill press, lathe, etc... although they might have a very good reason for these choices, I dont understand them myself.

3. The fact that you just find blueprints and research books by themselves in rooms, potentially very important ones at that that are tied to companion quests, makes me constantly worried about whether or not I missed something important in the previous room, like how I still don't have the recycling research for Mark and i'm still not sure if I missed it or its in the next room and with how glitchy this game can be at times I worry if I am ever missing important objects due to a glitch, but I am fully willing to admit that this very well might just be a me problem and i'm just slow.

Overall, I really like this game and I think if these smaller issues like the glitchy-ness were to ever be resolved it would massively improve the experience and make it even better than it currently is, and I really hope they stick with this game and fix these issues. There may have been some positives and negatives that I have missed, but this game was a very pleasant surprise and I personally really hope this game gets the recognition it deserves for being this honestly unique and pleasant to play through, when everything works properly, and I really hope these devs can continue to make these unique games.

this game isnt bad, it has a few things that bug me about it, like the fact, that's it full release, and probably should have come out as EA, because there are several improvements need to be made, such as if you place a block, and then suddenly run out of materials, you cant just come back and add to it, you have to pack it up and start again, no builder on the face of the earth, would do that. i would like to be able to clear away some of the static rubbish that's all over, it would make for a nicer place. someone keeps saying it's a cash grab, look if you dont like it, why are you even here. 

this game will never reach the heights of a AAA game, but that's ok. it is what it is, it's on sale right now, so if you have a keen interest in games that are more than blowing something up, or drving around in circles all day long, then you will surely enjoy this. 

am i recommending it, yeah sure. i've had no crashes, or major bugs.

This has been a solid game experience for me. Just as important for me has been the values expressed in the game. Some might take issue with this approach. However, all games express some set of values. From COD to WoW to Stardew Valley games express our assumptions about how the world does work and how we think it might or even should work.
It defenitily has a story to tell. Besides the story it is a pretty nice game. Haven't played much myself. But my friend who's further along in the game already built some pretty neat stuff.
Love this game! Has a few minor glitches, but nothing i can't work around

I have 15+ hours on the demo and 4+ hours on the actual game. I love the concept, vibes, and the no-stress factor where I can take my time to figure out stuff and not be pressured to do things. Now there are a few bugs that can be fixed in subsequent patches, I am sure: 
1. When building from a blueprint, if I add more of the wooden beam than needed, it just stays there without the option to pack it. It can only be packed the next time I log in. So that's surely an issue. 
2. Maybe provide an option for the wall and floor modules to be snapped and a key to make it not snap since the angles can get very difficult sometimes and lead to gaps between the floor and wall modules. 
3. A more comprehensive tutorial on the sheltering system and how I can make my community members more comfortable. 
4. Maybe provide a step-by-step to every mission to provide some kind of guideline, or else it isn't easy sometimes. 
5. Allow choosing of specific jobs to be done by the members. Like I want one member to precisely clear one set of debris, or build only rooms or build only chairs and tables etc. 
6. Maybe more choice of music. The West Wing music becomes annoying after the 2-hour mark! 

Re: Few more hours in and more bugs!!!
7. The characters do not do their duties after I have been playing the game non-stop for 7 hours. This is core to the gameplay since the economic and community aspect cannot be fulfilled without everybody fulfilling their roles. Getting more difficult to complete everything myself! So please fix this... 
8. Every time I assign beds, they are automatically getting unassigned!
9. Please provide more hints about how to do stuff!!!

I still love the game! I know more updates are coming and I support and love the dev for the awesome game you have created. I look forward to the match being further polished. Thank you so much for such a 'breath of fresh air' game. Its extremely original.
Really enjoying this game. Yes its a little buggy, but with time i hope devs fix the bugs.
Like the game, lots to do but as of today not able to play because after going to sleep at night the game freezes up. Hope they fix this issue soon.

I have only played 4.8hrs on steam but I have been playing this game on XB for free for over 25hrs.  Many people are saying that it is dry or not finished.  The game is just how the devs wanted it.  Its slow, dry, no hand holding.  The story is captivating and without that, the game would suck.  I normally don't play games that feel clunky so-to-speak but this game has me pulled in.  I want to know whats going to happen.  Methodically well thought out and well designed.  If you are considering it, understand that you will struggle in the beginning and you will get stuck here and there.  Like in real life, you have got to figure things out and things take time.  The game portrays this well.  This game is totally worth the money and worth giving a try.  If you are unsure if it is for you, go cloud game it from XB app first but you don't get the true feeling of the game from there as the game feels sluggish and drifty/accelerated on there which is why I bought it on steam.
a greate game could do with a bit of tlc but other then that amazing

I have played every builder under the sun. They have a variety of options and sometimes limited interactivity with a story. This game has a bit of both. It is primarily a game that teaches building techniques with its new system which has the potential to grow and generate new ideas within it. Then a backend narrative that offers up secret characters, hidden areas and a robust development that may empty the tissue box. For those that have said the beginning is a bit slow, I think you need to get past that point like any game. Do we actually need to be handed every single thing? For each person, progress is a different animal. The ramp up to any end game content can be slow, I mean look at the old EQ or Wow vanilla days. We all complain how we miss it and then doing a quest or camping that mob for 10 days is too much for our Tik Tok brains now. This game does make you earn it, and learn it. (Rem those old Commodore tool days? Oh, thats right. nvm.) There is love in this game. More than I can say about the garbage content on some of the MMOs now a days. It is not modular repetition. The story is not copy past kill x things or harvest x things. It has heart. You feel that which the characters feel. I throw my gauntlet in the ring to complete this game. Now who out there is up for that?

Multiplayer right now is just sandbox mode with invited friends, so that is a bit of a bummer. However, single player is far more fleshed out and offers you an interesting story and lots to do. While the tutorial can be confusing at times, it's good enough that with just a little playing around you can figure it out. Building is done like nothing I've seen before, piece by piece you get a blueprint to work off of. Sometimes multiple blueprints combine to finish a large project. The big projects can seem daunting, but are always rewarding when completed. I really enjoy this aspect of the game, and the quest that go along with them.  

You will gain the trust of other people as you help them, and they can be ordered around to help you achieve your goals. This is a community that needs leadership, and you've been chosen! Put them to work building components, farming, salvaging materials, or researching something in the tech tree. Economy wise, there is really much game there. It's just a build it, and then sell it style. Then you can use the money to get the components you need for the bigger or newer project. The basic materials will keep trickling in, so you can build as many chairs / chest as you want, and sell them to get money for those rare materials you need. 

For not  being an EA release, it feels like it should of been. It is missing some pieces and parts, you might say. Some assembly is required after purchase. Ok, enough bad puns.... There are tech tree skills space holders in place, and like I mentioned, the multiplayer / cooperative game is only sandbox, and a huge let-down imo. I want to build a community together with my friends. Or be put in a cooperative situation where we must work together to achieve xyz goals. I hope we get some more cooperative elements added in the future, as that was my main draw to Common'Hood. 

To conclude, single player is worth it, if you like integral builders with the rare interesting story included, then this is for you. Don't buy it for the cooperative or MP play, as you will be disappointed.

A relaxing building game with a huge amount of flexibility in its building tools and opportunities for creativity. Also features a positive story with some deep messages. 

While the game has opportunities to simplify the system, the current state is fun, engaging, and definitely worth to try. Looking forward to where the devs will take it!

I helped out on the beta round 2 for this game, and was really encouraged by the devs' responsiveness. I don't think this is a cash grab - if it were, they would have released way earlier and probably used a bunch of those Unity store assets we all know and love. I don't expect this game to be polished, and while the bugs are still more plentiful than a finished game should probably see, hopefully the dev team continues to work on this and add more content for those of us who pushed for permaculture in the endgame :-3

Having spent quite awhile with this game i can say i thoroughly enjoy it. The beta/demo periods have had it's issues and bugs but they've had swift fixes to them. The game still has some kinks that needs to be ironed out but once that gets done this is a perfect game for the people that kind of have nomadic ideas or feel like society as we know it today is too focused on things that doesn't matter.

And it's highly creative to build your own creations.

Recommended with a caveat... The building system is super awesome and versatile but is also pretty finicky and requires alot of grinding and work... so if your the kind of person like me that enjoys harvesting one million sticks, making various different sized planks and boards, then smacking them together to your hearts content... then this game is a definite must.

The cast of characters you meet are also pretty cool in their individuality and each has their own wants and needs that need to be met in order to get the most out of the tasks you assign them (think rimworld style scheduling). There is also a neat-o story of the beginnings of complete post capitalism collapse.

But seriously, you come for the seriously awesome building system. The fact that the building blueprints you find are completely optional, and you can make your own from scratch, or just freehand every board, plank, and panel is just really freeing when compared to most build-centric games.

These complaints on you have to pack things if you run out of mats is nonsense. I was building the chair and now the floor platforms and if i run out of mats I just stop and go to the planar make more and then come back to put it. Whats the problem?

And hours to figure out the research? Its right there on the video on the help. Which keeps blinking annoyingly. What more does that tutorial need?

I hope people didnt need to watch a youtube vid on how to water the potatoes just coz it didnt give you a watercan and theres no well

Hate that this game is getting so much negativity! I absolutely love the concept of being able to build everything from scratch and have a first person "community builder" type game. I know i haven't gotten into the real "meat" of the game yet, but so far it's been a blast!

I don't usually leave reviews, but I wanted to weigh in since my experience has been great. Yes it has some bugs, yes it has features I would have liked implemented differently, and yes it's missing some documentation, but honestly that's what I expected from a small development team who don't have a huge catalog. The team has been knocking out patch fixes and communicating within their Discord since they launched, thus far this has not been a drop it and abandon situation. 

I personally have enjoyed the challenge of needing to figure out what tool I need to find or what workshop I need to build next. I also played the Demo quite extensively before I bought it, so I knew what I was getting into. 

I do not regret my purchase, and with other 20+ hours sunk in I haven't run out of new spaces to explore or new things to build.

This game has a fantastic story and build to proceed process and game play.  The only disappointing part of the game is the inability to clean up and restore the factory and different areas.   Hopefully the story grows and develops to accommodate this ;)

I've really enjoyed the game so far, excited to see how the story progresses. Seems like there are a few bugs or odd little UI things that could be fixed at some point, but the architectural mechanics and environment are something I haven't seen before. I've been describing it to my friends as solarpunk minecraft. I know economy management is one of the main aspects (and I haven't progressed very far) but it would be cool if you could gradually shift to a gift economy or other mutual aid structures entirely instead of having dollars, but that's just a political nitpick given the focus on self sufficiency and community building. Tutorial could use some work for sure, but if you can manage without handholding I would absolutely recommend this game.

I think the negative reviews are exaggerated. Yes, I do have to set my resolution every time I play, but it's not game breaking, I'm sure it's something they can fix. Only having an issue with missions that don't give enough details, leaving me clueless. I had to check a Youtube video on how to fix the crane (had no clue where the control panel was, now I do). In my opinion those are things that can be fixed easily and shouldn't be a reason to thumb a game down. 

Issues such as frequent crashes, would be a reason to thumb a game down, but the game runs fine (1440p, 100+ fps on a 6900 XT). The art and ways of constructing are original. Put a few farm plots at the start and that will make your first money. I started with 20 plots and have enough left to sell, to buy parts that I need. The game is relaxed, no stress, you can  take whatever time you need.

I only got 4 hours in, but did play the beta a year ago for +-10 hours. Hard to say how far they got with the game since, but at this point I don't feel it's fair to down vote the game for none game breaking issues, So far, I'm enjoying it.

I have only reached Chapter 6 at the time of writing this review, when I started this Visual Novel I didn't expect much but it turned out better than what I expected.
It's a Kinetic novel so no choices or multiple endings, the story is not complex but simple and interesting one, players would want to know what happens next, it has a nice music and better English version than most Chinese VNs or FMVs I have played.
I am not a true fan of Visual Novels I just play them if I found it interesting this one turned interesting, when I started the VN it was slow paced for me but as the story progressed it was interesting, I liked the presentation of the story, background music is good as well.
It's a simple review don't want to spoil the story it's just to mention that the VN is good and English version is better than most Chinese VNs etc, it's nice they added an English.
I will update as I finish the VN.

This was my first kinetic novel, no choices to make, just read and think about some of the subjects that are thrown up in the story.  The reader can make their own mind about how deep or how seriously they want to take this. I found it easy to get involved with the characters, to understand the frustrations of trying to earn a living in a city where everyone lives the same boring life-style and where young people and their parents do not understand one another. The protagonist, a reluctant hero, goes for a walk  and meets a young girl one summer's night when his tv breaks down, and thats when it all starts as he has to  grow up and make life decisions that have consequences not just for himself, but other people. I can't say more without giving away the plot.
I enjoyed it. I liked the art style and while the music got a bit monotonous, its not intrusive and suits the story. It can be as deep and philosophical as you want, or the  complete opposite if you just want an interesting tale to entertain you, and there are enough twists to the plot to do that.
For less than the cost of a movie ticket you will get at least a double feature out of this novel, even better if its in a sale! So if you like tales that can make you think, its worth considering.
挺好的故事，UI很棒，美术可能缺点经费...

[b]Note:[/b] I received a Steam key to help with alpha testing although I had no involvement with the development.

[i]Snowdome[/i] is a kinetic visual novel developed by Lunatic Works and published by Lunatic Works and Kikai Digital.  You take on the role of Tan Yan, a young adult who works as a convenience store clerk in the city.  He lives his life day-to-day, feeling unappreciated and that life will never change.  He moved away from the country, as he didn't want to become a farmer like his parents before him.  One day, when Tan Yan decides to take a walk on the beach (because his TV is broken, so he can't watch his favorite show), he encounters a teenage girl.  She makes him an intriguing offer.  [i]Help me save the world.[/i]  This piques Tan Yan's interest and thus, his adventure begins.  As this is a kinetic novel, I'll stop my story synopsis here to avoid spoilers.  ^^

As for the gameplay, there isn't really much in [i]Snowdome[/i].  Since it is a kinetic novel, there are no choices, so you just play through the story from beginning to end.  There are a total of 14 chapters and an epilogue.  Of course, as this is a visual novel, there are plenty of CGs although no Steam achievements at the time of this review.  

Since I've gone over the story and gameplay, I'll move onto my critique.  Upon first playing [i]Snowdome[/i], I found myself drawn into the story.  Like Tan Yan, I was intrigued upon meeting the girl who claims she can help save the world.  At first, I couldn't help wondering what kind of story this would be.  Would it be supernatural, psychological, etc.?  Without trying to spoil, the story is quite complicated.  It will make the reader think and wonder what's real.  Also, what's happening to the people on the island where Tan Yan is?  Although it seemingly appears peaceful, there's definitely way more to it.  I especially loved the plot twists that happen very late in the story, which (again) made me think.  It's really difficult to explain what I loved about this story without spoiling certain parts, but I will say that it'll make you think a lot.  In regards to the writing, I felt that it was well-paced.  At times, I noticed some run-on sentences although the translation itself is more than passable.  It's easy to follow the story.  In regards to art, I liked the backgrounds.  They almost feel real at times and I liked the sprites.  The effects used were really well done, and the CGs were beautifully drawn.  As for the soundtrack, I enjoyed listening to the various tracks.  They give off that mysterious vibe that is prevalent throughout the story.  Oh, and there's also Chinese voice acting (except for Tan Yan).  It's relaxing listening to the voices while reading the story at the same time.

Overall, would I recommend [i]Snowdome[/i]?  If you're looking for a visual novel with an in-depth story, then yes, I would.  As I've mentioned, [i]Snowdome[/i] will make you think a lot, which I personally believe is a good thing.  XD  I wish it had Steam achievements, but this is my only negative (and a minor one at that).  For the asking price, I'd say it's worth getting, as it took me less than 9.5 hours to complete, so there's plenty of story.  However, if you'd rather wait for a bargain, there are sales, of course.  Even at full price, it's a good deal to get this game.  ^^

I found it easy to get involved with the characters, to understand the frustrations of trying to earn a living in a city where everyone lives the same boring life-style and where young people and their parents do not understand one another. The protagonist, a reluctant hero, goes for a walk  and meets a young girl one summer's night when his tv breaks down, and thats when it all starts as he has to  grow up and make life decisions that have consequences not just for himself, but other people. I can't say more without giving away the plot.
I enjoyed it. I liked the art style and while the music got a bit monotonous, its not intrusive and suits the story. It can be as deep and philosophical as you want, or the  complete opposite if you just want an interesting tale to entertain you, and there are enough twists to the plot to do that.
For less than the cost of a movie ticket you will get at least a double feature out of this novel, even better if its in a sale! So if you like tales that can make you think, its worth considering.
挺好的故事，UI很棒，美术可能缺点经费...

[i]Snowdome[/i] is a kinetic visual novel developed by Lunatic Works and published by Lunatic Works and Kikai Digital. He moved away from the country, as he didn't want to become a farmer like his parents before him.  One day, when Tan Yan decides to take a walk on the beach (because his TV is broken, so he can't watch his favorite show), he encounters a teenage girl.  She makes him an intriguing offer.  [i]Help me save the world.[/i]  This piques Tan Yan's interest and thus, his adventure begins.  As this is a kinetic novel, I'll stop my story synopsis here to avoid spoilers.  ^^

As for the gameplay, there isn't really much in [i]Snowdome[/i].  Since it is a kinetic novel, there are no choices, so you just play through the story from beginning to end.  There are a total of 14 chapters and an epilogue.  Of course, as this is a visual novel, there are plenty of CGs although no Steam achievements at the time of this review.  

Since I've gone over the story and gameplay, I'll move onto my critique.  Upon first playing [i]Snowdome[/i], I found myself drawn into the story.  Like Tan Yan, I was intrigued upon meeting the girl who claims she can help save the world.  At first, I couldn't help wondering what kind of story this would be.  Would it be supernatural, psychological, etc.?  Without trying to spoil, the story is quite complicated.  It will make the reader think and wonder what's real.  Also, what's happening to the people on the island where Tan Yan is?  Although it seemingly appears peaceful, there's definitely way more to it.  I especially loved the plot twists that happen very late in the story, which (again) made me think.  It's really difficult to explain what I loved about this story without spoiling certain parts, but I will say that it'll make you think a lot.  In regards to the writing, I felt that it was well-paced.  At times, I noticed some run-on sentences although the translation itself is more than passable.  It's easy to follow the story.  In regards to art, I liked the backgrounds.  They almost feel real at times and I liked the sprites.  The effects used were really well done, and the CGs were beautifully drawn.  As for the soundtrack, I enjoyed listening to the various tracks.  They give off that mysterious vibe that is prevalent throughout the story.  Oh, and there's also Chinese voice acting (except for Tan Yan).  It's relaxing listening to the voices while reading the story at the same time.

Overall, would I recommend [i]Snowdome[/i]?  If you're looking for a visual novel with an in-depth story, then yes, I would.  As I've mentioned, [i]Snowdome[/i] will make you think a lot, which I personally believe is a good thing.  XD  I wish it had Steam achievements, but this is my only negative (and a minor one at that).  For the asking price, I'd say it's worth getting, as it took me less than 9.5 hours to complete, so there's plenty of story.  However, if you'd rather wait for a bargain, there are sales, of course.  Even at full price, it's a good deal to get this game.  ^^

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Being an avid fan of the Gabriel Knight series and knowing where the Markus Ritter series has drawn its biggest inspiration from, Ghosts of the Past honours the GK series well with a good storyline and backstories which helps give the GK series a universe-like feel. The most impressive thing about this game is the screens where dialogue between characters are involved. The subtle movements adds another dimesion and quality of FMV games today. The gameplay is good for moderate adventure gamers and there's some well-inserted sometimes dry humour, which makes it fun, especially when stuck on something. Much care and consideration of locations, set decorations and camera work can be seen in this game as well. I love learning new languages, so it's great that this game allows you to play the game with audio in one language and subtitles in another language. Overall, this game is wunderbar and I can't wait to see what happens next!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Just finished it, took me about 6 hours. So the game is rather short, but a well-rounded FMV gaming experience. Designed with great attention to detail. The makers call it "queer cheesy trash" - so "queer" is true, "cheesy" is true, "trash" rather less so. Of course, the story is full of clichés, but it still manages to create believable characters whose dialogues are well-recorded by the speakers. Occasional overacting included. The puzzles are mostly simple, but not simplistic. Here and there I found the solution a little "random", but perhaps that changes if you read the text snippets more carefully... All in all, the game clearly plays in the premier league of FMV adventure games alongside modern classics such as "Late Shift" and "Contradiction", although it is much more of a traditional point & click than a quick-time click orgy, fortunately there are no QTEs in the gameplay. A clear recommendation!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
As a massive Gabriel Knight fan, I at first had a chuckle when I heard of this game. It's so obvious that it's a fan game. But I've also been missing those games for decades, so I knew I had to play it anyway. 

Well, turns out it's good on its own. Though not without its bugs and navigation issues, the game features  interesting characters, locations, and mechanics. It also distances itself from GK in a lot of ways, particularly with the antagonists. While in GK the 'shadows' can be any sort of supernatural beast, in Markus Ritter they are more like a species of body snatchers.

Overall, an enjoyable experience. Hopefully we see more.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Wow wow wow! That was absolutely amazing. I can honestly say that this was the best game I've played in years. The drama is non-stop, there is just the right amount of challenge and no puzzle outstays its welcome - as there is a task list on the phone. The musical score is again really strong and always apt, though no big standout this time like the main song in the first installment as well as that beautiful piano piece. The places to visit and explore are interesting and a couple of the houses are amazing. It's refreshing to have a bisexual main character with a strong romantic and emotional focus on his current partner. Ludwig looks like a cute pixie / elf. There is a lot of fun in this game too - I loved reading the quirky astrology section in the daily newspaper and there are some sudden, unexpected laugh out loud moments too. There are also sprinklings of 90's point and click game references throughout, not least an article in the newspaper about holidaying on Monkey Island. In terms of tech requirements, I did get a little lag in places, most notably the club/cafe (just a short section)  but it was no game-breaker and I played it on a very old desktop computer with an i3 processor. The FMV is really crisp and high resolution. The huge effort and work that has clearly gone into the production of this game is very evident. Anyone who loves 90's style FMV games (often regarded as the golden age of point and click) will also love this game as it's up there with the best of those while being for a modern audience with modern values. Just brilliant!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: This game was made with a lot of love and attention to detail. Lots of hidden nods and eastereggs that point to various point & click classics. It's amazing to see a game set in my home city. Just  started out and hope to dive deeper into the adventures of a new generation of Schattenjäger! :) We will catch the killer!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
I very much enjoyed both demo's and looked forward to the full game. I am not far advanced into the game as yet but, having enjoyed Gabriel Knight, I of course like the story line , characters, humour, and atmosphere.  
It is also visually appealing.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
As a huge fan of FMV games, I jumped at the chance to become a Kickstarter backer for Ghosts of the Past after really enjoying The Lost Family. I wasn’t disappointed – this release has become one of my favourite results from a crowdfunding project.

While the storyline is quite serious, Ghosts of the Past has a dry sense of humour running throughout it which had us laughing at times. Adventure gamers will notice the Gabriel Knight influences and happily recognise the references to the classics. Unlike recent FMV games which seem to focus on choice and consequence, this plays more like a point-and-click. The puzzles aren’t particularly challenging but they’re enjoyable and don’t outstay their welcome.

Seeing how Markus’s character has grown between the first and second game was a treat. He starts off as someone who’s drifting along without direction, and he now has it after delving into his family’s history. I won’t say too much for fear of spoiling it, but the end of Ghosts of the Past sets him up to develop further and show a stronger side to his personality.

On that note, well done to the cast of actors! FMV games tend to be over-the-top and, although there are some appropriately campy moments here, everyone does an excellent job of making their characters believable. I wasn’t fond of Ro at the beginning, but after she drops her guard, you can see why she’s a great counterpart for Markus.

If there’s ever a Kickstarter campaign for a third Markus Ritter instalment, sign me up already.

Our playthrough can be watched here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3uD5pqZX-WI&list=PLUZJQ9yOnp3k8mDWZbB7MQb-apVggpn_v

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: A fun FMV game with beautiful locations. Very interested to see where the story goes next.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
Aside from this one and Flimmersoft's first title "Markus Ritter: The Lost Family", I actually have not played other games of this genre - yet. With their first game they caught my attention and with this one they got me on boat. They've sucessfully managed to foster interest for this type of games in me. Although I can't say - for my own lack of experience with these kinda games - where this one truly ranks when directly compared to other names on the playing field, I still very much enjoyed spending time with it. I'd still give them only 0/100 points possible, for they dared teasing me with a food recipe while I was hungry. :(

I'm definitely gonna check out the games that inspired the people over at Flimmersoft to create this, if only to make the wait for their next game feel shorter.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: very campy, very fun, very gay

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: So far, it's a fun fmv :)

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Being an avid fan of the Gabriel Knight series and knowing where the Markus Ritter series has drawn its biggest inspiration from, Ghosts of the Past honours the GK series well with a good storyline and backstories which helps give the GK series a universe-like feel. The most impressive thing about this game is the screens where dialogue between characters are involved. The subtle movements adds another dimesion and quality of FMV games today. The gameplay is good for moderate adventure gamers and there's some well-inserted sometimes dry humour, which makes it fun, especially when stuck on something. Much care and consideration of locations, set decorations and camera work can be seen in this game as well. I love learning new languages, so it's great that this game allows you to play the game with audio in one language and subtitles in another language. Overall, this game is wunderbar and I can't wait to see what happens next!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Just finished it, took me about 6 hours. So the game is rather short, but a well-rounded FMV gaming experience. Designed with great attention to detail. The makers call it "queer cheesy trash" - so "queer" is true, "cheesy" is true, "trash" rather less so. Of course, the story is full of clichés, but it still manages to create believable characters whose dialogues are well-recorded by the speakers. Occasional overacting included. The puzzles are mostly simple, but not simplistic. Here and there I found the solution a little "random", but perhaps that changes if you read the text snippets more carefully... All in all, the game clearly plays in the premier league of FMV adventure games alongside modern classics such as "Late Shift" and "Contradiction", although it is much more of a traditional point & click than a quick-time click orgy, fortunately there are no QTEs in the gameplay. A clear recommendation!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
As a massive Gabriel Knight fan, I at first had a chuckle when I heard of this game. It's so obvious that it's a fan game. But I've also been missing those games for decades, so I knew I had to play it anyway. 

Well, turns out it's good on its own. Though not without its bugs and navigation issues, the game features  interesting characters, locations, and mechanics. It also distances itself from GK in a lot of ways, particularly with the antagonists. While in GK the 'shadows' can be any sort of supernatural beast, in Markus Ritter they are more like a species of body snatchers.

Overall, an enjoyable experience. Hopefully we see more.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: Wow wow wow! That was absolutely amazing. I can honestly say that this was the best game I've played in years. The drama is non-stop, there is just the right amount of challenge and no puzzle outstays its welcome - as there is a task list on the phone. The musical score is again really strong and always apt, though no big standout this time like the main song in the first installment as well as that beautiful piano piece. The places to visit and explore are interesting and a couple of the houses are amazing. It's refreshing to have a bisexual main character with a strong romantic and emotional focus on his current partner. Ludwig looks like a cute pixie / elf. There is a lot of fun in this game too - I loved reading the quirky astrology section in the daily newspaper and there are some sudden, unexpected laugh out loud moments too. There are also sprinklings of 90's point and click game references throughout, not least an article in the newspaper about holidaying on Monkey Island. In terms of tech requirements, I did get a little lag in places, most notably the club/cafe (just a short section)  but it was no game-breaker and I played it on a very old desktop computer with an i3 processor. The FMV is really crisp and high resolution. The huge effort and work that has clearly gone into the production of this game is very evident. Anyone who loves 90's style FMV games (often regarded as the golden age of point and click) will also love this game as it's up there with the best of those while being for a modern audience with modern values. Just brilliant!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: This game was made with a lot of love and attention to detail. Lots of hidden nods and eastereggs that point to various point & click classics. It's amazing to see a game set in my home city. Just  started out and hope to dive deeper into the adventures of a new generation of Schattenjäger! :) We will catch the killer!

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
I very much enjoyed both demo's and looked forward to the full game. I am not far advanced into the game as yet but, having enjoyed Gabriel Knight, I of course like the story line , characters, humour, and atmosphere.  
It is also visually appealing.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
As a huge fan of FMV games, I jumped at the chance to become a Kickstarter backer for Ghosts of the Past after really enjoying The Lost Family. I wasn’t disappointed – this release has become one of my favourite results from a crowdfunding project.

While the storyline is quite serious, Ghosts of the Past has a dry sense of humour running throughout it which had us laughing at times. Adventure gamers will notice the Gabriel Knight influences and happily recognise the references to the classics. Unlike recent FMV games which seem to focus on choice and consequence, this plays more like a point-and-click. The puzzles aren’t particularly challenging but they’re enjoyable and don’t outstay their welcome.

Seeing how Markus’s character has grown between the first and second game was a treat. He starts off as someone who’s drifting along without direction, and he now has it after delving into his family’s history. I won’t say too much for fear of spoiling it, but the end of Ghosts of the Past sets him up to develop further and show a stronger side to his personality.

On that note, well done to the cast of actors! FMV games tend to be over-the-top and, although there are some appropriately campy moments here, everyone does an excellent job of making their characters believable. I wasn’t fond of Ro at the beginning, but after she drops her guard, you can see why she’s a great counterpart for Markus.

If there’s ever a Kickstarter campaign for a third Markus Ritter instalment, sign me up already.

Our playthrough can be watched here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3uD5pqZX-WI&list=PLUZJQ9yOnp3k8mDWZbB7MQb-apVggpn_v

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text: A fun FMV game with beautiful locations. Very interested to see where the story goes next.

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
Aside from this one and Flimmersoft's first title "Markus Ritter: The Lost Family", I actually have not played other games of this genre - yet. With their first game they caught my attention and with this one they got me on boat. They've sucessfully managed to foster interest for this type of games in me. Although I can't say - for my own lack of experience with these kinda games - where this one truly ranks when directly compared to other names on the playing field, I still very much enjoyed spending time with it. I'd still give them only 0/100 points possible, for they dared teasing me with a food recipe while I was hungry. :(

I'm definitely gonna check out the games that inspired the people over at Flimmersoft to create this, if only to make the wait for their next game feel shorter.
